Diagnosis of common skin cancers
  • Home
  • Show All Code
  • Hide All Code
  • Download Rmd

Diagnosis of common skin cancers

Follow-up remarks for 2025-03-10 meeting

Francois Collin

  • 1 Introduction
  • 2 Data QC - Sample and Run Validity Criteria
    • 2.1 On controls
  • 3 Model Selection
  • 4 Assay Lock
  • 5 NCI IOM Guidelines
  • 6 Discovery Phase Workflows
    • 6.1 Recommended Workflow
    • 6.2 No-Confirmation Workflow
    • 6.3 Iterative Model Build Up Workflow
    • Validation Figures
  • 7 References


1 Introduction

  • These remarks are offered at the planning stage of a formal validation study. Before that planning can usefully proceed, it is worth being explicit about what validation is and is not:
    • Validation verifies the established performance of a finalized, locked procedure - going into validation we must be confident in the level of the procedure’s performance.

  • Having sound QC procedures tuned to the features of the test input data, reliable inclusion/exclusion criterion, and a well understood model selection process are necessary to ensure that the test performance is stable and reproducible.

  • The history of omics-based diagnostic development offers well-documented examples of flawed products successfully meeting the requirements of the validation runs, and making it into the clinical settings. This is precisely why the NCI commissioned the IOM review that produced the Evolution of Translational Omics guidelines.

  • As an aside, it is not a well appreciated fact that statistical reasoning is helpful in the analysis of data all along the data processing pipelines of omic platforms. I spend a significant amount of time making sure that normalization works as expected, and that poor quality samples are flagged to alert the downstream analysis modules.


2 Data QC - Sample and Run Validity Criteria

  • Before anything else happens, the nature of the data used by the test being developed needs to be fully understood. A data QC procedure customized to the specific requirements of the test in development should be finalized and locked before being applied to runs that are part of the test development.

  • The samples which are used to fit and validate a model are in many cases more impactful on the ultimate performance of an omic-based test than the selected model itself. It is therefore critically important to apply accurate, statistically sound Inclusion/Exclusion criteria when selecting samples for development and validation (FDA (2019) [1]).

  • The success of this selection or identification process depends on having established a comprehensive QC procedure, customized to the specific needs of the test under consideration. The metrics in Melania et al. (2019) [2] to assess normalization can also be used for QC.

  • Through analysis we can determine empirically which are the best metrics to use to identify which specimens and analytical runs are to be excluded. Inclusions and exclusions must be pre-specified as part of the locked procedure.

  • This process must be fully documented.


2.1 On controls

  • There are various sorts:
    • samples: positive and negative differential expression controls
    • features (miRNAS): stably expressed
    • synthetic miRNA spike-ins from commercial

  • Control strategies can be implemenred such that various truths are built in every run and can be summuned for QC and normalization.


3 Model Selection

  • The model selection process should be fully documented to ensure reproducibility.

  • The criteria used to score models must be clearly stated as part of the documentation.
    • the model with the highest cross-validated accuracy estimate is not necessarily the best model to choose
      • many models may achieve the same accuracy after accounting for estimation error
      • other factors may be equally important:
        • interpretability
        • reproducibility
        • robustness to fitting set selection
        • in a multinomial context, not all error are equal

  • In a multi-site context it is important to have a representative set of sites in the fitting set.


4 Assay Lock

A key component of a diagnostic test development process is Assay Lock.

  • Validation only starts after assay lock.

  • Lock means that the complete end-to-end procedure is frozen. It must occur before any validation begins

  • The scope includes: specimen handling, instrument acquisition, and all data processing from the instruments output to the final result

  • Every step must be fully documented at lock
    • this includes how the model was selected, all QC metrics and procedures, and acceptance/inclusion criteria.

  • Any post-lock change constitutes a protocol deviation, and may invalidate subsequent validation data



5 NCI IOM Guidelines

  • Following several cases of flawed products making it into the clinic, the National Cancer Institute (NCI) requested that the Institute of Medicine (IOM) form a committee to develop recommendations to clarify and improve the pathway from discovery to first use of omics-based tests in a clinical trials.

This report identifies best practices to enhance development, evaluation, and translation of omics-based tests while simultaneously reinforcing steps to ensure that these tests are appropriately assessed for scientific validity before they are used to guide patient treatment in clinical trials.

  • Links to handy resources:
    • White paper - Evolution of Translational Omics: Lessons Learned and the Path Forward
      • Guidelines for the Development of Omic Based Products

  • Summary slides


6 Discovery Phase Workflows

For the discovery phase, there are three common development workflows.


6.1 Recommended Workflow

The recommended workflow is depicted in Figure 6.1.

CAPTION <-  paste(
c("Omics-based test development process: recommended discovery phase"))

Figure 6.1: Omics-based test development process: recommended discovery phase


Note on the Confirmation Step

  • In the flowchart in Figure 6.1, the recommended analysis steps includes a confirmation step which is run prior to any formal validation run.

  • The confirmation step can provide added confidence in the expected results from the validation runs, or may indicate that some tweak to the test procedure is needed.

  • The use of a confirmation step is highly recommended unless the test data has been extensively studied in the past and there would be little to learn from the results.
    • in my experience the need for a separate confirmation step can be assessed on a case by case basis.
    • if the training data are complex and representative of the target population, and the model well understood, and the out-of-fold error rate similar to the test subset error rate, then the risk of encountering unexpected and divergent results at validation is limited.


6.2 No-Confirmation Workflow

  • The No-Confirmation workflow is depicted in Figure 6.2.

  • The No-Confirmation workflow can be risky, unless the test data are familiar.

itw

CAPTION <-  paste(
c("Omics-based test development process: no-confirmation discovery phase workflow"))

Figure 6.2: Omics-based test development process: no-confirmation discovery phase workflow


6.3 Iterative Model Build Up Workflow

The Iterative Model Build Up workflow is depicted in Figure 6.3.

Figure 6.3: Omics-based test development process: no-confirmation discovery phase workflow


Validation Figures

  • Comments


7 References

1.
Food, U.S., and Administration., D. (2026). Considerations for design, development, and analytical validation of next generation sequencing (NGS)–based in vitro diagnostics (IVDs) intended to aid in the diagnosis of suspected germline diseases: Guidance for stakeholders and food and drug administration staff. Available at: https://collections.nlm.nih.gov/catalog/nlm:nlmuid-101734092-pdf.
2.
Molania, R., Gagnon-Bartsch, J.A., Dobrovic, A., and Speed, T.P. (2019). A new normalization for nanostring nCounter gene expression data. Nucleic Acids Res 47, 6073–6083.
